mission.title: Install and Configure MetalLB (Bare-metal Load Balancer)
mission.description: MetalLB is a load-balancer implementation for bare metal Kubernetes clusters, utilizing standard routing protocols. It is particularly useful in environments where cloud load balancers are not available. Key trade-offs include the need for manual configuration of IP address ranges and potential complexity in managing network routes.

steps.title: Add MetalLB Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official MetalLB Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add metallb https://metallb.github.io/metallb

```

> This command sets up the repository for installing MetalLB.

steps.title: Install MetalLB Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are necessary for MetalLB to function correctly as they define the custom resources used by MetalLB.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/metallb/metallb/v0.15.3/manifests/namespace.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/metallb/metallb/v0.15.3/manifests/metallb.yaml

```

> Creates the namespace for MetalLB.
> Deploys the MetalLB components.

steps.title: Install MetalLB using Helm
steps.description:
This step installs MetalLB in the Kubernetes cluster using the Helm package manager.

```bash

helm install metallb metallb/metallb --namespace metallb-system --create-namespace --version 0.15.3

```

> Installs MetalLB in the metallb-system namespace.

steps.title: Create MetalLB ConfigMap
steps.description:
This step creates a ConfigMap that defines the IP address pool for MetalLB to use.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  namespace: metallb-system
  name: config
data:
  config: |
    address-pools:
    - name: default
      protocol: layer2
      addresses:
      - 192.168.1.200-192.168.1.250
EOF

```

> Adjust the IP range according to your network.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  namespace: metallb-system
  name: config
data:
  config: |
    address-pools:
    - name: default
      protocol: layer2
      addresses:
      - 192.168.1.200-192.168.1.250
EOF

steps.title: Verify MetalLB Installation
steps.description:
This step checks if MetalLB is running correctly by listing the pods in the metallb-system namespace.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n metallb-system

```

> You should see MetalLB controller and speaker pods running.

uninstall.title: Uninstall MetalLB
uninstall.description:
This step removes MetalLB from your cluster. Be aware that this will delete all associated resources and may lead to data loss if services depend on MetalLB.

```bash

helm uninstall metallb --namespace metallb-system

kubectl delete namespace metallb-system

```

> Uninstalls the MetalLB release.
> Removes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade MetalLB
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your configuration. This step includes draining any workloads if necessary, upgrading MetalLB, and verifying the installation.

```bash

kubectl get configmap config -n metallb-system -o yaml > metallb-config-backup.yaml

helm upgrade metallb metallb/metallb --namespace metallb-system --version 0.15.3

kubectl get pods -n metallb-system

```

> Backup current MetalLB configuration.
> Upgrades MetalLB to the specified version.
> Verify that all MetalLB pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get configmap config -n metallb-system -o yaml > metallb-config-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to allocate IP address'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the specified IP range is exhausted or incorrectly configured.

**Fix:**
Check the ConfigMap for the correct IP address range and ensure there are available IPs.

troubleshooting.title: MetalLB pods are in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This could be due to misconfiguration in the ConfigMap or missing permissions.

**Fix:**
Review the logs of the MetalLB pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n metallb-system' and correct any configuration errors.

troubleshooting.title: LoadBalancer service is not getting an external IP
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if the MetalLB speaker pods are not running or not able to communicate with the Kubernetes API.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the MetalLB speaker pods are running and check their logs for errors.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Service not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the service is not correctly defined or if MetalLB is not configured to handle it.

**Fix:**
Verify that the service type is set to LoadBalancer and that MetalLB is properly configured.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for IPv6 addressing, improved error messages for configuration issues.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v0.14, ensure that your address pools are compatible with the new IPv6 support.

resolution.summary: You should see the MetalLB pods running with the command 'kubectl get pods -n metallb-system' showing 'Running' status for both controller and speaker.
codeSnippets:

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:

data:

    address-pools:
    - name: default

EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.25 or higher and the necessary tools installed.
